title: LinkedIn post 05 - Detecting downward non-linearity
text:
In chemical calibration, non-linearity usually bends downward.
It often looks like the beginning of saturation: the signal still increases with concentration, but the slope starts to decrease. If we force a straight line through that response, high-level samples can become biased.
I simulated a mild downward curvature and compared the same 15-measurement designs:

15 levels measured once
5 levels measured in triplicate
3 levels measured five times

The designs are not equally good at detecting curvature.
Replicates are excellent for estimating precision at selected levels. But they do not create information between levels. If the analytical question is “is the response really linear?”, then the number and placement of concentration levels are critical.
This is the trade-off I keep coming back to: calibration design depends on the question.
Want repeatability? Use replicates.
Want shape information? Use levels across the range.
Want both? Then the design has to admit that both goals need measurement budget.
Linearity is not proven by a nice-looking straight line through too few levels.
#AnalyticalChemistry #Chemometrics #Calibration #RStats

title: LinkedIn post 03 - Weighted least squares
text:
Weighted least squares is not a magic upgrade from ordinary least squares.
It is a way of saying: some calibration points are measured more precisely than others, and the regression should know that.
If a method has approximately constant relative standard deviation, the absolute standard deviation increases with concentration. In that case, inverse-variance weighting often makes sense. This is why analytical chemists often see weights like 1/x or 1/x².
But these are not just formulas. They are assumptions about the measurement process.
In my simulation, 1/x² behaved well when the data really had constant RSD. That is expected because variance is then roughly proportional to concentration squared.
The important part is the word “when”.
Using 1/x² because it is familiar is not the same as showing that the method has that variance structure. Empirical weights can help, but only if the calibration design includes enough replicated levels to estimate variance.
This is where design and statistics meet. If we want to estimate precision from calibration data, we need replicates. If we want to describe curve shape, we need levels.
Same injections. Different information.
#AnalyticalChemistry #Chemometrics #RStats #WeightedLeastSquares

title: LinkedIn post 01 - Calibration design
text:
Calibration is not only a question of how many standards we prepare. It is also a question of what we want those standards to teach us.
I simulated a simple case: linear response, constant absolute measurement error, and 15 total calibration measurements. Then I compared three designs:

15 levels measured once
5 levels measured in triplicate
3 levels measured five times

Same budget. Different information.
The 15-level design gives more information about the shape of the calibration line. The replicated designs give more information about repeatability at selected concentrations. Both are useful, but they answer different analytical questions.
This is the point I would like more analysts to discuss before running the experiment: are we trying to estimate a slope, estimate repeatability, check linearity, or support uncertainty around unknown samples?
A calibration design is not just a regulatory checkbox. It is an experimental design choice.
In the next step I looked at what happens when the response remains linear, but the variance is no longer constant. That is where the usual OLS calibration starts to become less comfortable.
#AnalyticalChemistry #Chemometrics #RStats #Calibration

title: When the weighting model is only partly true
text:
The realistic complication
A clean constant-RSD model is useful for learning WLS, but real low-level analytical work often behaves differently. Near the LOQ, the response standard deviation may stop decreasing because blank noise, integration noise, or background correction dominates. Farther from the LOQ, relative error can become more stable.
This mixed behavior creates a problem for simple global weighting rules. A rule such as \(1/x^2\) assumes that variance continues to shrink rapidly as concentration approaches zero. If the method has a low-concentration noise floor, that assumption can give too much influence to the lowest calibrators.


Setup


Code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)

source(here("R", "design_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "weight_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "fit_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "inversion_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "metrics_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "plotting_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "prediction_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "response_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "simulation_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "variance_functions.R"))




Code
set.seed(321)

mean_fun <- f_linear
sigma_fun <- function(x) {
  sigma_loq_mixed(
    x,
    mean_fun = mean_fun,
    sigma_floor = 4,
    rsd = 0.07
  )
}

designs <- list(
  "15 x 1" = design_15x1(1, 100),
  "5 x 3" = design_5x3(1, 100),
  "3 x 5" = design_3x5(1, 100)
)




The mixed variance pattern


Code
variance_grid <- data.table(
  x = seq(1, 100, length.out = 300)
)

variance_grid[, response := mean_fun(x)]
variance_grid[, sigma := sigma_fun(x)]
variance_grid[, rsd := sigma / response]

ggplot(variance_grid, aes(x = x, y = sigma)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "Concentration",
    y = "Response standard deviation"
  ) +
  theme_bw()





A mixed model: constant absolute error near the low end, then approximately constant RSD.




Code
ggplot(variance_grid, aes(x = x, y = 100 * rsd)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "Concentration",
    y = "RSD (%)"
  ) +
  theme_bw()





A mixed model: constant absolute error near the low end, then approximately constant RSD.




The standard deviation has a floor at low concentration, while the RSD becomes more stable only after moving away from the low end. This is a familiar analytical situation: the method behaves differently near the quantification limit than it does in the comfortable middle of the range.


Code
weight_grid <- data.table(
  x = seq(1, 100, length.out = 300)
)

weight_plot_data <- rbindlist(
  list(
    data.table(
      x = weight_grid$x,
      method = "True mixed variance",
      weight = 1 / sigma_fun(weight_grid$x)^2
    ),
    data.table(
      x = weight_grid$x,
      method = "1/x",
      weight = weights_x(weight_grid$x)
    ),
    data.table(
      x = weight_grid$x,
      method = "1/x2",
      weight = weights_x2(weight_grid$x)
    )
  )
)

plot_weights(weight_plot_data)





Simple global weighting rules do not fully match the mixed variance model.




The true weight curve flattens near the low end because precision does not keep improving indefinitely. The \(1/x^2\) curve keeps rising. That mismatch is exactly where routine low-level calibration can become fragile.


Fit and test models


Code
calibration_data <- lapply(
  designs,
  simulate_dataset,
  mean_fun = mean_fun,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)

models <- lapply(
  calibration_data,
  function(dt) {
    list(
      "OLS" = fit_ols(dt),
      "WLS true" = fit_wls_true(dt),
      "WLS estimated" = fit_wls_empirical(dt),
      "WLS 1/x" = fit_wls_x(dt),
      "WLS 1/x2" = fit_wls_x2(dt)
    )
  }
)

test_data <- generate_test_set(
  n = 1000,
  xmin = 1,
  xmax = 100,
  mean_fun = mean_fun,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)

prediction_tables <- lapply(
  models,
  function(model_set) {
    lapply(
      model_set,
      predict_calibration_model,
      test_data = test_data,
      variance_fun = sigma_fun
    )
  }
)




Code
performance_table <- rbindlist(
  lapply(
    names(prediction_tables),
    function(design_name) {
      data.table(
        design = design_name,
        model = names(prediction_tables[[design_name]]),
        rmse = sapply(prediction_tables[[design_name]], metric_rmse),
        coverage = sapply(prediction_tables[[design_name]], metric_coverage),
        mean_interval_width = sapply(prediction_tables[[design_name]], metric_mean_pi_width)
      )
    }
  )
)

kable(
  performance_table,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Global prediction performance under a mixed variance model."
)



Global prediction performance under a mixed variance model.

A global average can hide the region that matters most. For LOQ-related applications, the low concentration region deserves its own view.


Code
region_summary <- rbindlist(
  lapply(
    names(prediction_tables),
    function(design_name) {
      rbindlist(
        lapply(
          names(prediction_tables[[design_name]]),
          function(model_name) {
            dt <- copy(prediction_tables[[design_name]][[model_name]])
            dt[, region := cut(
              x_true,
              breaks = c(0, 10, 40, Inf),
              labels = c("Low", "Middle", "High")
            )]
            dt[, .(
              design = design_name,
              model = model_name,
              rmse = sqrt(mean(error^2)),
              mean_abs_relative_error = mean(abs(relative_error)),
              coverage = mean(x_true >= x_lpl & x_true <= x_upl)
            ), by = region]
          }
        )
      )
    }
  )
)

kable(
  region_summary,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Performance by concentration region. Weight mismatch is most visible near the low end."
)

Take-home message
WLS requires a variance model. A simple global rule may be convenient and may even be approximately right over part of the range, but that does not make it right near the LOQ. When the method has a low-concentration noise floor, the analyst should inspect low-level residuals, replicate behavior, and prediction performance separately from the global calibration summary.

title: Calibration curves are not only about linearity
text:
The hidden assumption
A calibration curve can be perfectly linear and still be statistically uncomfortable. The reason is that OLS assumes more than a straight mean response. It also assumes that the response variability is approximately constant across the calibration range.
Many analytical methods do not behave that way. At low levels, signal processing and background noise may dominate. At higher levels, the absolute scatter can increase with response. The result is heteroscedasticity: the variance changes with concentration.
This episode keeps the response linear and changes only the measurement variability. That separation is useful for analysts because it shows that linearity and constant variance are different questions.


Setup


Code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)

source(here("R", "design_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "weight_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "fit_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "inversion_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "metrics_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "plotting_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "prediction_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "response_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "simulation_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "variance_functions.R"))




Code
set.seed(123)

mean_fun <- f_linear
sigma_fun <- function(x) {
  sigma_constant_rsd(
    x,
    mean_fun = mean_fun,
    rsd = 0.10
  )
}

designs <- list(
  "15 x 1" = design_15x1(1, 100),
  "5 x 3" = design_5x3(1, 100),
  "3 x 5" = design_3x5(1, 100)
)




The variance pattern


Code
variance_grid <- data.table(
  x = seq(1, 100, length.out = 200)
)

variance_grid[, sigma := sigma_fun(x)]
variance_grid[, rsd := sigma / mean_fun(x)]

ggplot(variance_grid, aes(x = x, y = sigma)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "Concentration",
    y = "Response standard deviation"
  ) +
  theme_bw()





Under constant RSD, absolute response standard deviation increases with concentration.




The response is still linear, but the scatter is not constant. A high-concentration point is expected to have a larger absolute residual than a low-concentration point. Treating those residuals as equally informative is the assumption that we will challenge in the next episode.


Simulate OLS calibrations


Code
calibration_data <- lapply(
  designs,
  simulate_dataset,
  mean_fun = mean_fun,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)

models <- lapply(
  calibration_data,
  fit_ols
)




Code
plot_design(
  calibration_data[["15 x 1"]],
  mean_fun
)





The calibration can look linear even when variance clearly changes with concentration.






Code
plot_residuals(models[["15 x 1"]])





Residuals from OLS show the kind of pattern analysts should inspect.




A residual plot is not decoration. It is a check of whether the model is describing the measurement process. If the residual spread grows with fitted response, the calibration line may still be linear, but the OLS uncertainty model is no longer appropriate.


Predict unknown samples


Code
test_data <- generate_test_set(
  n = 1000,
  xmin = 1,
  xmax = 100,
  mean_fun = mean_fun,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)

prediction_tables <- lapply(
  models,
  predict_calibration_model,
  test_data = test_data,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)




Code
performance_table <- data.table(
  design = names(prediction_tables),
  rmse = sapply(prediction_tables, metric_rmse),
  coverage = sapply(prediction_tables, metric_coverage),
  mean_interval_width = sapply(prediction_tables, metric_mean_pi_width),
  heteroscedasticity_detected = sapply(models, metric_hetero_detected)
)

kable(
  performance_table,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "OLS prediction performance when absolute variance changes with concentration."
)



OLS prediction performance when absolute variance changes with concentration.









design
rmse
coverage
mean_interval_width
heteroscedasticity_detected




15 x 1
5.803
0.960
23.496
FALSE


5 x 3
5.954
0.985
24.038
TRUE


3 x 5
5.702
0.952
22.222
TRUE





The diagnostic test is intentionally simple, and with only 15 measurements it will not detect every heteroscedastic calibration. That is a practical lesson in itself. A small calibration experiment may not contain enough information to estimate a variance pattern reliably, even when that pattern is real.


Take-home message
Linearity is not the whole calibration story. A method can have a straight mean response and still require a variance-aware analysis. When residual spread changes across the range, the next question is not whether the calibration is linear, but whether every point should have the same influence on the fitted line.

title: Calibration Design Simulations
text:
This repository explores calibration design choices for chemical analysis using small, reproducible R simulations.
The examples are written for analytical chemists, R users, and chemometricians who want to reason about calibration beyond the fitted line. The central question is how a fixed calibration budget should be distributed when the analyst cares about prediction error, uncertainty, weighting, low-level behavior, and evidence of linearity.

title: Calibration Design Simulations
text:
Source articles

Calibration design: how should we spend 15 measurements?
Calibration curves are not only about linearity
When variability changes: introducing weighted least squares
When the weighting model is only partly true
Can calibration designs detect small downward non-linearity equally well?

title: Calibration Design Simulations
text:
LinkedIn drafts
Short copy-paste drafts are stored separately in posts/ so the computational articles and social posts can evolve independently.

title: Calibration design: how should we spend 15 measurements?
text:
The practical question
In routine chemical analysis, calibration is often discussed as a question of how many levels are required. That is a necessary question, but it is not the only one. If the total number of measurements is fixed, the analyst must also decide whether those measurements should be used to cover more concentrations or to replicate fewer concentrations more carefully.
In this first episode we use a deliberately friendly calibration problem. The response is linear, the absolute measurement error is constant across the range, and ordinary least squares is the correct model. That simplicity is useful because it lets us focus on the design question without mixing it with weighting, curvature, or LOQ effects.
We compare three ways of spending 15 calibration measurements: 15 levels measured once, five levels measured in triplicate, and three levels measured five times. Each design has the same measurement budget, but the information content is different.


Setup


Code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)

source(here("R", "design_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "weight_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "fit_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "inversion_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "metrics_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "plotting_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "prediction_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "response_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "simulation_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "variance_functions.R"))




Code
set.seed(123)

mean_fun <- f_linear
sigma_fun <- sigma_homo

designs <- list(
  "15 x 1" = design_15x1(1, 100),
  "5 x 3" = design_5x3(1, 100),
  "3 x 5" = design_3x5(1, 100)
)




What the designs contain


Code
design_summary <- rbindlist(
  lapply(
    names(designs),
    function(name) {
      dt <- designs[[name]]
      data.table(
        design = name,
        measurements = nrow(dt),
        concentration_levels = uniqueN(dt$x),
        replicates_per_level = paste(sort(unique(as.integer(table(dt$x)))), collapse = ", ")
      )
    }
  )
)

kable(
  design_summary,
  digits = 2,
  caption = "All designs use 15 total measurements, but they spend them differently."
)



All designs use 15 total measurements, but they spend them differently.


design
measurements
concentration_levels
replicates_per_level




15 x 1
15
15
1


5 x 3
15
5
3


3 x 5
15
3
5







Code
design_plot_data <- rbindlist(
  lapply(
    names(designs),
    function(name) {
      dt <- copy(designs[[name]])
      dt[, design := name]
      dt
    }
  )
)

plot_design_comparison(design_plot_data)





The same measurement budget can be spent on many levels or on replicated levels.




The design with 15 levels gives many positions along the calibration curve. The replicated designs give fewer positions, but they say more about repeatability at those selected concentrations. Neither choice is automatically superior; each one answers a slightly different analytical question.


Simulate and fit one calibration experiment


Code
calibration_data <- lapply(
  designs,
  simulate_dataset,
  mean_fun = mean_fun,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)

models <- lapply(
  calibration_data,
  fit_ols
)




Code
for (title in names(calibration_data)) {
  print(
    plot_design(calibration_data[[title]], mean_fun) +
      labs(title = paste0("Design ", title))
  )
}





One simulated calibration for each design. The line is the true response.







One simulated calibration for each design. The line is the true response.







One simulated calibration for each design. The line is the true response.




In this scenario OLS is appropriate because the response standard deviation is constant. This matters: OLS is not being used because it is the default button in software, but because its assumptions match the simulated measurement process.


Test the calibration on unknown samples
A calibration curve is useful only when it predicts unknown samples. We therefore simulate future samples, invert the fitted calibration model, and compare the estimated concentration with the known simulated truth.


Code
test_data <- generate_test_set(
  n = 1000,
  xmin = 1,
  xmax = 100,
  mean_fun = mean_fun,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)

prediction_tables <- lapply(
  models,
  predict_calibration_model,
  test_data = test_data,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)




Code
performance_table <- data.table(
  design = names(prediction_tables),
  rmse = sapply(prediction_tables, metric_rmse),
  mean_absolute_error = sapply(prediction_tables, metric_mae),
  bias = sapply(prediction_tables, metric_bias),
  coverage = sapply(prediction_tables, metric_coverage),
  mean_interval_width = sapply(prediction_tables, metric_mean_pi_width)
)

kable(
  performance_table,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Prediction performance for one simulated calibration experiment."
)



Prediction performance for one simulated calibration experiment.










design
rmse
mean_absolute_error
bias
coverage
mean_interval_width




15 x 1
5.115
4.045
-0.661
0.974
23.200


5 x 3
5.207
4.172
1.353
0.970
22.886


3 x 5
5.282
4.160
-1.390
0.968
23.021





The exact ranking changes from simulation to simulation, so this table should not be read as a universal league table. The important point is that, under a truly linear response with constant absolute error, the 15-level design often performs well because it estimates the slope and intercept using information spread across the full range. Replicates improve information about repeatability, but they do not add as much new information about the line shape when the line is already simple.


Code
for (title in names(prediction_tables)) {
  print(
    plot_prediction_error(prediction_tables[[title]]) +
      labs(title = paste0("Design ", title))
  )
}





Relative concentration error across the calibration range.







Relative concentration error across the calibration range.







Relative concentration error across the calibration range.




The plots show relative error because that is usually closer to how analytical chemists evaluate performance. The same absolute concentration error is much more important near the low end of the range than near the high end. Even in this friendly OLS case, the low range deserves careful interpretation.


Take-home message
This first simulation does not prove that 15 unreplicated levels are always best. It shows something more practical: a calibration design is an experimental design choice. With the same 15 measurements, we can learn more about curve shape or more about repeatability, but not both equally well.
If the main question is the slope over a wide range, many levels help. If the main question is repeatability, replicates help. If the main question is linearity, the placement and number of levels become critical.

title: When variability changes: introducing weighted least squares
text:
Why weights enter the story
When the response variability changes with concentration, ordinary least squares still fits a line, but it fits that line as if every point had the same response variance. Weighted least squares changes the question. Instead of treating all residuals equally, it gives more influence to observations expected to be more precise.
For approximately constant relative standard deviation, the response standard deviation is roughly proportional to concentration. The variance is then roughly proportional to concentration squared, which explains the common analytical rule \(w = 1/x^2\).
That rule is not magic. It is an assumption about the measurement process.


Setup


Code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)

source(here("R", "design_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "weight_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "fit_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "inversion_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "metrics_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "plotting_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "prediction_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "response_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "simulation_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "variance_functions.R"))




Code
set.seed(123)

mean_fun <- f_linear
sigma_fun <- function(x) {
  sigma_constant_rsd(
    x,
    mean_fun = mean_fun,
    rsd = 0.10
  )
}

designs <- list(
  "15 x 1" = design_15x1(1, 100),
  "5 x 3" = design_5x3(1, 100),
  "3 x 5" = design_3x5(1, 100)
)




Compare weight functions


Code
weight_grid <- data.table(
  x = seq(1, 100, length.out = 200)
)

weight_plot_data <- rbindlist(
  list(
    data.table(
      x = weight_grid$x,
      method = "True inverse variance",
      weight = 1 / sigma_fun(weight_grid$x)^2
    ),
    data.table(
      x = weight_grid$x,
      method = "1/x",
      weight = weights_x(weight_grid$x)
    ),
    data.table(
      x = weight_grid$x,
      method = "1/x2",
      weight = weights_x2(weight_grid$x)
    )
  )
)

plot_weights(weight_plot_data)





Different weighting rules encode different assumptions about variance.




A steeper weight curve gives much more importance to the low concentration standards. That may be appropriate under constant RSD, but it becomes risky if the low end has a noise floor. The important question is therefore not whether a weighting rule is familiar, but whether it matches the method.


Fit OLS and WLS models


Code
calibration_data <- lapply(
  designs,
  simulate_dataset,
  mean_fun = mean_fun,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)

models <- lapply(
  calibration_data,
  function(dt) {
    list(
      "OLS" = fit_ols(dt),
      "WLS true" = fit_wls_true(dt),
      "WLS estimated" = fit_wls_empirical(dt),
      "WLS 1/x" = fit_wls_x(dt),
      "WLS 1/x2" = fit_wls_x2(dt)
    )
  }
)


Empirical WLS requires replicated concentration levels. With 15 unreplicated levels, the calibration experiment has no direct way to estimate the variance at each level, so the empirical approach falls back to OLS. That is not a coding detail; it is an experimental design consequence.


Prediction performance


Code
test_data <- generate_test_set(
  n = 1000,
  xmin = 1,
  xmax = 100,
  mean_fun = mean_fun,
  variance_fun = sigma_fun
)

prediction_tables <- lapply(
  models,
  function(model_set) {
    lapply(
      model_set,
      predict_calibration_model,
      test_data = test_data,
      variance_fun = sigma_fun
    )
  }
)




Code
performance_table <- rbindlist(
  lapply(
    names(prediction_tables),
    function(design_name) {
      data.table(
        design = design_name,
        model = names(prediction_tables[[design_name]]),
        rmse = sapply(prediction_tables[[design_name]], metric_rmse),
        bias = sapply(prediction_tables[[design_name]], metric_bias),
        coverage = sapply(prediction_tables[[design_name]], metric_coverage),
        mean_interval_width = sapply(prediction_tables[[design_name]], metric_mean_pi_width)
      )
    }
  )
)

kable(
  performance_table,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Prediction performance under constant relative measurement error."
)



Prediction performance under constant relative measurement error.

coverage

The true-variance WLS fit is the benchmark because it uses information that is available only in simulation. The simple \(1/x^2\) rule should be close when the constant-RSD assumption is true. The \(1/x\) rule is a milder correction, and empirical weighting depends strongly on whether the design contains enough replicates to estimate variability.


Code
for (design in names(prediction_tables)) {
  for (model in names(prediction_tables[[design]])) {
    print(
      plot_prediction_error(prediction_tables[[design]][[model]]) +
        labs(title = paste(design, model))
    )
  }
}





Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.







Relative prediction error by design and model.






Take-home message
Weighted least squares is useful because it connects calibration fitting with analytical precision. It is not a universal upgrade over OLS. If the weights describe the real variance structure, WLS can improve prediction and uncertainty. If the weights are only a habit, they can move the calibration in the wrong direction.

title: Can calibration designs detect small downward non-linearity equally well?
text:
The chemical calibration problem
In chemical calibration, non-linearity usually does not appear as a curve that bends upward forever. More often, the response begins to bend downward as the system approaches saturation, detector limitations, matrix effects, or the early part of a plateau.
That direction matters. A downward bend means that high-concentration samples produce less signal than a purely linear extrapolation from the low range would suggest. If the analyst forces a straight line through that response, concentration estimates can become biased, especially near the high end.
This episode asks whether the three 15-measurement designs detect small downward non-linearity equally well. The answer is expected to depend on both the number of distinct concentration levels and the measurement-error pattern.


Setup


Code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)

source(here("R", "design_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "weight_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "fit_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "inversion_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "metrics_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "plotting_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "prediction_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "response_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "simulation_functions.R"))
source(here("R", "variance_functions.R"))




Code
set.seed(456)

mean_fun <- function(x) {
  f_saturation_quadratic(
    x,
    slope = 1,
    curvature = 0.0008
  )
}

sigma_relative <- function(x) {
  sigma_constant_rsd(
    x,
    mean_fun = mean_fun,
    rsd = 0.08
  )
}

curvature_detected <- function(dt, weights = NULL, alpha = 0.05) {
  if (is.null(weights)) {
    m_linear <- lm(y ~ x, data = dt)
    m_quad <- lm(y ~ x + I(x^2), data = dt)
  } else {
    m_linear <- lm(y ~ x, data = dt, weights = weights)
    m_quad <- lm(y ~ x + I(x^2), data = dt, weights = weights)
  }

  p_value <- anova(m_linear, m_quad)$`Pr(>F)`[2]

  isTRUE(p_value < alpha)
}

designs <- list(
  "15 x 1" = design_15x1(1, 100),
  "5 x 3" = design_5x3(1, 100),
  "3 x 5" = design_3x5(1, 100)
)




The downward curvature


Code
curve_grid <- data.table(
  x = seq(1, 100, length.out = 300)
)

curve_grid[, y_curved := mean_fun(x)]
curve_grid[, y_linear_reference := x]
curve_grid[, difference_pct := 100 * (y_curved - y_linear_reference) / y_linear_reference]

ggplot(curve_grid, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y_curved), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y_linear_reference), linetype = 2) +
  labs(
    x = "Concentration",
    y = "Response"
  ) +
  theme_bw()





The simulated response bends downward, like the early stage of saturation.




Code
ggplot(curve_grid, aes(x = x, y = difference_pct)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "Concentration",
    y = "Deviation from linear response (%)"
  ) +
  theme_bw()





The simulated response bends downward, like the early stage of saturation.




The dashed line is the ideal linear response. The solid line remains increasing, but its slope decreases with concentration. This is the small and clinically familiar danger zone: the calibration can still look almost linear, while the high end is already beginning to lose response.


One noisy calibration


Code
example_data <- simulate_dataset(
  design_15x1(1, 100),
  mean_fun,
  sigma_homo
)

plot_design(example_data, mean_fun)





A single noisy calibration may not make mild downward curvature obvious.




Visual inspection is useful, but it is not enough. A small downward bend can be hidden by normal analytical scatter, especially if the design has few distinct concentration levels.


Repeated simulation
For each simulated calibration, we compare a linear model with a quadratic model. When response error is constant, we use an unweighted comparison. When relative error is constant, we use inverse-variance weights so that the curvature test matches the error structure.


Code
n_sim <- 500

simulate_detection <- function(design_name, design_fun, error_case) {
  detections <- replicate(
    n_sim,
    {
      if (error_case == "Constant absolute error") {
        dt <- simulate_dataset(
          design_fun(1, 100),
          mean_fun,
          sigma_homo
        )

        curvature_detected(dt)
      } else {
        dt <- simulate_dataset(
          design_fun(1, 100),
          mean_fun,
          sigma_relative
        )

        curvature_detected(
          dt,
          weights = 1 / dt$sigma^2
        )
      }
    }
  )

  data.table(
    design = design_name,
    error_case = error_case,
    detection_rate = mean(detections)
  )
}

design_functions <- list(
  "15 x 1" = design_15x1,
  "5 x 3" = design_5x3,
  "3 x 5" = design_3x5
)

detection_results <- rbindlist(
  lapply(
    names(design_functions),
    function(name) {
      rbindlist(
        list(
          simulate_detection(
            name,
            design_functions[[name]],
            "Constant absolute error"
          ),
          simulate_detection(
            name,
            design_functions[[name]],
            "Constant relative error"
          )
        )
      )
    }
  )
)

kable(
  detection_results,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Estimated probability of detecting mild downward curvature."
)



Estimated probability of detecting mild downward curvature.


design
error_case
detection_rate




15 x 1
Constant absolute error
0.088


15 x 1
Constant relative error
0.174


5 x 3
Constant absolute error
0.108


5 x 3
Constant relative error
0.132


3 x 5
Constant absolute error
0.102


3 x 5
Constant relative error
0.126







Code
ggplot(
  detection_results,
  aes(
    x = design,
    y = detection_rate,
    fill = error_case
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    width = 0.7
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0.05,
    linetype = 2
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Calibration design",
    y = "Curvature detection rate",
    fill = "Error structure"
  ) +
  theme_bw()





Detection probability depends on the design and on the error structure.




The dashed line marks the false-positive rate expected from a 5% test when no curvature exists. Detection rates above that line indicate that the design has some power to see the downward bend.


Interpretation
The designs are not equally informative about shape. The 15-level design sees the curve at many positions and is therefore better suited to detecting gradual curvature. The five-level design has fewer positions but still gives some view of the bend. The three-level design can estimate repeatability at selected points, but it has limited information about what happens between them.
This is the central trade-off. Replicates improve precision estimates, but they do not create new concentration levels. If the analytical question is linearity, the number and placement of levels are critical.


Take-home message
In chemical calibration, small non-linearity should usually be imagined as downward curvature or the beginning of saturation. A design built mainly around replication may be useful for precision, but it is not automatically strong for detecting that curvature. The calibration plan should state explicitly whether the priority is repeatability, weighting, low-level uncertainty, or evidence of linearity.

title: LinkedIn post 02 - Heteroscedasticity
text:
A calibration curve can be linear and still have a statistical problem.
The hidden assumption behind ordinary least squares is not only that the mean response is linear. It is also that the response variability is approximately constant across the range.
Many analytical methods do not behave like that. At higher concentrations, the absolute signal scatter can increase even when the relative precision is acceptable.
That is heteroscedasticity.
In a simulation with constant RSD, the calibration line still looked linear. But the residual spread increased with concentration. This matters because OLS treats all residuals as equally informative. A large residual at high concentration may simply be expected measurement variability, while a smaller residual at low concentration may be analytically more important.
This is why residual plots are not decorative. They are part of understanding the measurement process.
For me, the practical question is not only: “is the calibration linear?”
It is also: “are all calibration points equally precise?”
If the answer is no, weighted least squares may be more appropriate, but only if the weights reflect the real variance pattern.
#AnalyticalChemistry #Chemometrics #Calibration #RStats

title: LinkedIn post 04 - Weight mismatch near LOQ
text:
Simple weighting rules can fail exactly where analytical chemists care most: near the LOQ.
A clean constant-RSD assumption says that variance keeps decreasing as concentration decreases. Under that assumption, 1/x² weighting is reasonable.
But near the low end, many methods have a noise floor. Blank variability, integration noise, and background correction do not vanish just because the concentration is small.
So the real variance pattern may be mixed:

constant absolute error near the LOQ
approximately constant relative error farther from the LOQ

In that situation, a global 1/x² weight can give too much influence to the lowest calibration points. Those points may not be as precise as the weight function assumes.
The fitted line can still look acceptable. The problem shows up in low-level prediction error and interval coverage.
My practical takeaway: do not judge weighting only by the global calibration plot or by R². Look at residuals, low-level recovery, replicate behavior, and uncertainty by concentration region.
Weights should describe the method, not decorate the regression.
#AnalyticalChemistry #LOQ #Chemometrics #RStats
